Prerequisite: Experiment 1 validated and committed. Branch off it: git checkout experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent && git checkout -b experiment/decorations-2a-guides-overlay. Read docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md and docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md (Experiment 2 section) first.

Build a ViewPlugin that, after each debounced recompute (gate on docChanged || viewportChanged, NOT unconditionally on every ViewUpdate — the prior attempt's per-ViewUpdate recompute was more expensive than needed), reads already-rendered pixel positions via view.coordsAtPos()/view.lineBlockAt() and draws one absolutely- positioned overlay <div> per ancestor level, inline-styled (el.style.top/left/height set directly from JS, not via CSS classes) into a layer living outside .cm-content — this is obsidian-outliner's proven technique (VerticalLines.ts in its source), not something to reinvent from scratch; read that file if you want the exact reference shape. Handle the out-of-band mode-toggle refresh with a dedicated StateEffect rather than an empty view.dispatch({}) forcing a full recompute path.

Test against every fixture in e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts, in both themes. The specific case to get right that was never even attempted last time: the multi-line/wrapped continuation fixture — a guide line must span the full rendered height of a multi-line node (should come free from lineBlockAt, which is block-level and covers all wrapped visual rows — verify this rather than assuming it). npm run build:plugin/vault:install before any real-vault check. Record results in the comparison table.
I'll start by checking git state, creating the branch, and reading the required background docs.

1	# Postmortem: the `outline-decorations` change (2026-07-13)
2	
3	**Verdict: failed experiment.** After three full redesign cycles, extensive automated
4	testing (198 unit tests, 33 e2e tests, all green), and multiple rounds of screenshot
5	verification, the feature was still visibly broken in real vault use: marker size
6	scaling with heading font-size, inconsistent indentation, wrong continuation-line
7	indentation, markers overlapping native bullets, misplaced guide lines. This document
8	records what was tried, why each attempt failed, and — the more important part — why
9	the testing approach used throughout kept producing false confidence. It's written for
10	whoever plans the next iteration (possibly a future session of the author of this one).
11	
12	## What the change was trying to do
13	
14	Give outline mode a visual language: headings, paragraphs, list items, and code/table/
15	quote/callout blocks should all read as nodes in one tree, with indentation that reflects
16	tree depth (not raw markdown encoding) and a marker prominent enough to make the
17	structure legible at a glance — motivated by a dev-vault finding that flat, paragraph-
18	heavy documents gave no visual signal that outline mode was even active. See
19	[04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md)'s "Visual layer is now the testability
20	bottleneck" note and the archived `outline-decorations` OpenSpec change for the original
21	ask.
22	
23	## Chronological account
24	
25	### Attempt 1: paragraph-only decoration, guide lines via fixed-position CSS
26	
27	Scope: only paragraph nodes got a `::before` marker; a separate `::before` line drew a
28	vertical "guide" at a fixed screen offset. List items, headings, and atoms were left
29	completely untouched.
30	
31	Shipped, all tasks marked done, verified via synthetic e2e fixtures (clean headings/
32	lists/paragraphs, no checkboxes) and two screenshots. Two real bugs were caught during
33	that verification round (both by screenshot, not by the DOM-attribute e2e assertions,
34	which passed the whole time):
35	
36	- The `padding-left` CSS rule needed a more specific selector *and* `!important` to take
37	  effect at all — a plain `.to-node-line { padding-left: ... }` was silently overridden
38	  by Obsidian's own later, equal-specificity `.cm-line` rules, even on lines with no
39	  competing native padding of their own.
40	- The rule read `var(--list-indent, 2em)` for the per-depth unit. CSS custom properties
41	  resolve at the point of *use*, not declaration — so the `em` resolved against each
42	  line's own font-size, and a heading (larger font) indented more per depth-level than a
43	  paragraph at the identical tree depth. Fixed with a plugin-local fixed `rem` constant.
44	
45	Both fixes were verified by re-screenshotting the *same two synthetic fixtures* and
46	declared done. Nothing with checkboxes, real nesting, or code blocks was tried yet.
47	
48	### User report: broken in the real vault
49	
50	Two problems, both invisible in the synthetic fixtures:
51	
52	1. `styles.css` wasn't even loading — `vault:install` symlinked `manifest.json` and
53	   `main.js` but not `styles.css`. Fixed, but this alone should have been a signal that
54	   the verification loop (screenshot the sandboxed e2e vault) was disconnected from what
55	   the user was actually running.
56	2. Once loaded: indentation "off, sometimes negative"; guide lines "all over the place,"
57	   including apparently when outline mode was off.
58	
59	### Attempt 2: unilateral pivot to "leave list items alone entirely"
60	
61	Root cause of "negative" indentation: Obsidian pairs a negative `text-indent` with
62	`padding-left` to hang a list item's bullet outside the text column. The `!important`
63	override touched only `padding-left`, leaving the native (smaller, per-raw-level)
64	`text-indent` in place — the mismatched pair put content in an arbitrary, sometimes
65	overlapping position. The guide line's fixed screen offset (attempt 1) also never scaled
66	with depth, so at real nesting it landed nowhere near the actual text.
67	
68	Response: stopped decorating list items, headings, and atoms altogether — only
69	paragraphs got padding or a marker — and dropped the guide-line feature entirely.
70	
71	**This was the first process failure of the session**: a scope reduction (dropping the
72	whole point of the change — one visual language across kinds) made and shipped without
73	checking with the user first. Called out directly: *"how come you decided to change the
74	design mid-implementation and didn't even stop to check with me?"*
75	
76	### User rejects the narrowed scope; redirects to a specific reference
77	
78	Explicit requirements: indentation must be *consistent relative to text* across every
79	block type; a *prominent, uniform "fat bullet"* marker (Logseq-style) across kinds, with
80	per-kind glyph variation an acceptable later refinement, not a blocker. Pointed at
81	`obsidian-outliner`'s `BetterListsStyles`/Vertical Indentation Lines as a concrete
82	reference implementation to study before writing more code.
83	
84	Research via DeepWiki on `vslinko/obsidian-outliner` surfaced the actual technique, which
85	this session had not been using:
86	
87	- Guide lines are **not** CSS. A `ViewPlugin` calls `view.coordsAtPos()`/`lineBlockAt()`
88	  to read *already-rendered* pixel positions (after Obsidian's own list math has run),
89	  then draws absolutely-positioned overlay `<div>`s at those measured coordinates. It
90	  never fights the box model — it measures the result and draws on top of it.
91	- Bullet restyling targets the **existing** native bullet DOM element
92	  (`.list-bullet::after`) rather than adding a new one, and is list-only — the plugin
93	  never needs to make a heading or paragraph imitate a list bullet, because vanilla
94	  Obsidian never indents those regardless of depth. That "make non-list kinds match list
95	  indentation" problem is unique to this project's universal-tree model; there was no
96	  existing-plugin precedent to lift for it.
97	- Its own native indent-guide dashes are explicitly disabled (`content: none` on
98	  `.cm-indent::before`) via a mode class when its custom guides are on, to avoid double
99	  rendering.
100	
101	Given the choice between "make everything match native list styling" and "make list
102	styling match everything else" (author's framing), the answer was explicit: unify by
103	owning the scheme, not by trying to reverse-engineer Obsidian's per-theme list metrics.
104	
105	### The `!important` warning already on file
106	
107	`docs/research/02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md` documents a case study
108	(`workflowy-style-outline`) whose from-scratch UI shipped **~890 uses of `!important`**
109	fighting Obsidian's own CSS, flagged as the majority of its community-scorecard lint
110	violations. This was already in the project's own research before this session started
111	and should have been weighed *before* reaching for `!important` at all, not discovered
112	as a justification after the fact. It turned out to matter far sooner than the "add a
113	settings UI later" scale the case study implies: this session needed `!important` for
114	literally three properties (`padding-left`, `margin-left`, `text-indent`) and still
115	produced a chain of regressions.
116	
117	### Attempt 3: unify all kinds via Obsidian's own hang mechanism
118	
119	Design: instead of fighting the `text-indent`/`padding-left` pair, own both halves
120	together, uniformly, for every kind — `padding-left: depth × unit`,
121	`text-indent: -1 × hang`, applied as one coherent rule to every decorated line (not just
122	paragraphs). A synthetic `::before` marker on kinds without a native one rides the same
123	hang naturally (pseudo-elements participate in `text-indent`'s first-line shift, so no
124	absolute-position math needed there). List items keep their native marker glyph and
125	position; only its `font-size`/`font-weight` are touched.
126	
127	First verification round found nothing wrong — because it was run against a stale
128	plugin bundle. **`npm run build` only runs `tsc --noEmit`; it does not invoke esbuild.**
129	The actual `main.js` Obsidian loads still had the previous (paragraph-only) code. This
130	was caught only by directly inspecting computed styles and noticing list items carried
131	none of the new CSS classes at all — a second, independent build-pipeline mistake
132	(the same category of "test wasn't actually testing the current code" failure, just at
133	the build layer instead of the vault layer).
134	
135	After an actual rebuild, computed-style checks showed correct, consistent per-depth
136	padding (0/24/48/72px) across headings, paragraphs, and list items. Screenshots of a
137	mixed journal fixture (checkboxes, nested lists) looked right — except code blocks
138	still didn't visibly indent.
139	
140	**Root cause**: `padding-left` shifts an element's own *content*; it never moves the
141	element's own border/background box. Invisible for plain text (no visible box to reveal
142	the gap), but a code fence renders a background box whose edges are unaffected by
143	padding — confirmed via `getBoundingClientRect()`: the background box's `left` was
144	identical to its container's `left` regardless of the line's computed `padding-left`.
145	Fix: atoms (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr) get `margin-left` instead, which actually
146	moves the box.
147	
148	### Attempt 3b: unifying bullet *shape*, not just size
149	
150	Restyling the native bullet glyph in place (bigger font-size on the existing "-"
151	character) left bullets looking like bold dashes next to round "●" markers on paragraphs
152	and headings — a shape mismatch. Attempted fix: hide the native dash
153	(`color: transparent`) and draw a matching "●" via `::after`, positioned absolutely.
154	
155	This collided with a native Obsidian element never previously accounted for: the
156	**fold-indicator** (collapse chevron), rendered for any list item that has children. Its
157	wrapper element has zero width in normal flow, but its child icon (~31px) visually
158	extends past that zero-width anchor regardless of the surrounding indentation scheme —
159	so it always intrudes on whatever renders immediately after it. Verified by directly
160	measuring both elements' `getBoundingClientRect()`s and confirming the ~9px overlap
161	persisted even after changing the hang unit — because the fold-indicator's icon size is
162	independent of `--to-hang`; shrinking the hang just moved both anchors together, not
163	their relative overlap.
164	
165	Attempted fix: a fixed `left` offset on the `::after` dot to clear the fold-indicator.
166	This **made it worse** — the offset needed to clear the icon pushed the dot far enough
167	right to overlap the item's own text (visibly cutting off the first letter: "users"
168	rendered as "●sers"). The dot and the text both anchor near the same point; a flat pixel
169	offset that clears one native element pushes into the next thing in flow, because the
170	underlying mechanism (inline flow position, not independent absolute placement) doesn't
171	allow moving one without the other.
172	
173	Reverted to the safer, non-repositioning version: resize the *existing* glyph in place,
174	touch nothing about its position. This can't collide with the fold-indicator or overlap
175	text because it never moves anything — it accepts "bullets read as bold dashes, not
176	matching dots" as a known, deferred cosmetic gap (which the user had already flagged as
177	acceptable to defer, distinct from the indentation-consistency requirement which was
178	not negotiable).
179	
180	### Final user report: still broken
181	
182	Despite the above — full green test suite, multiple rounds of screenshot verification
183	against increasingly realistic fixtures (checkboxes, deep nesting, mixed ordered/bullet
184	lists, code fences) — real use in the actual vault still showed:
185	
186	- **Bullets get bigger depending on the heading size.** A real, self-evident bug this
187	  session never caught: the marker's `font-size` was set in `em` (`0.6em` for the
188	  synthetic marker, `1.15em` for the resized native glyph) — the *exact same class of
189	  mistake* already identified and fixed for *indentation* (em resolves against each
190	  line's own font-size) was never applied to the *marker size* rule. A heading's larger
191	  base font inflates its marker proportionally; a paragraph's or a deeply nested list
192	  item's marker stays small. This should have been caught by the same reasoning that
193	  caught the first em/rem bug — it wasn't, because that fix was verified narrowly
194	  (indentation values only) rather than by re-auditing every other `em`-based rule in
195	  the same stylesheet for the same class of bug.
196	- **Indentation still "all over the place"** in real use, despite computed-style checks
197	  showing correct, consistent depth-based padding in the tested fixtures. Either the
198	  verified fixtures didn't cover the actual failure modes present in a real, long-lived
199	  vault (different note shapes, different nesting patterns, interaction with the user's
200	  actual theme), or there are further interactions with Obsidian's own CSS not yet
201	  identified.
202	- **List node continuations (Shift+Enter multi-line nodes) don't get the right
203	  indentation.** Never actually screenshotted or visually checked — the implementation
204	  assumption (continuation lines get the same `padding-left` as their node's first line,
205	  just without the `text-indent` hang) was asserted in code and in the unit tests'
206	  line-fact computation, but never verified against a real rendered multi-line list item
207	  or paragraph. A real, plausible gap in coverage, not a confirmed root cause.
208	- **List markers drawn on top of the dashes.** This directly contradicts the last known
209	  implementation state (resize-in-place, no overlay `::after`) and was never
210	  independently investigated before this document was written — the two live
211	  possibilities are (a) the user was testing against a stale build (this session's
212	  build pipeline had already caused two prior false-negative verification rounds for
213	  exactly this reason), or (b) a real decoration-application bug — e.g., stale/duplicate
214	  entries surviving across a `DecorationSet` recompute or a toggle-off/on cycle — that
215	  was never looked into.
216	- **Guide lines completely misplaced.** The vertical guide-line feature was dropped
217	  after attempt 2 and never reintroduced in this session's final implementation, so this
218	  report likely refers to the marker/indentation confusion above rather than a literal
219	  guide line — but it wasn't reconciled with the user before this document was written.
220	
221	## Why the testing approach kept producing false confidence
222	
223	This is the most important section for planning the next attempt — the bugs above are
224	specific to a CSS-override strategy, but the *verification gaps* are a repeatable
225	pattern that will recur under a different implementation strategy too if not addressed
226	directly.
227	
228	1. **DOM-attribute assertions test that code ran, not that it looks right.** The e2e
229	   suite checked CSS class names and the `--to-depth` custom property were present and
230	   correctly valued — and passed at every stage, including stages where the rendered
231	   result was visibly broken. Presence of an attribute says nothing about the resulting
232	   visual layout once cascade, specificity, and Obsidian's own rules are in the mix.
233	
234	2. **Screenshots caught real bugs — but the screenshot fixtures were never adversarial
235	   enough, and were checked for the *specific* thing being fixed, not for regressions
236	   elsewhere.** Each round screenshotted a fixture built to demonstrate the fix just
237	   made, not a fixed, growing regression corpus covering every previously-verified
238	   scenario. A fix for fold-indicator clearance was checked against fold-indicator
239	   clearance; it was not cross-checked against the earlier "does text stay readable"
240	   scenario, so the text-overlap regression it introduced wasn't caught until a
241	   dedicated screenshot of that specific case.
242	
243	3. **Marker *size* was never scrutinized, only marker *presence/shape/position*.** Every
244	   screenshot taken in this session had headings and paragraphs with visibly different
245	   marker sizes sitting right next to each other — the data needed to catch the em/rem
246	   marker bug was in nearly every screenshot from attempt 3 onward. It wasn't caught
247	   because the reviewer (this session) was looking for "is there a marker, is it round,
248	   is it positioned correctly," not "is it the same size everywhere."
249	
250	4. **The build pipeline was a repeat source of false negatives.** `npm run build`
251	   (`tsc --noEmit`) does not rebuild `main.js`; only `npm run build:plugin` (esbuild)
252	   does. At least twice in this session, CSS/behavior was "verified" against a stale
253	   bundle, producing a confident but wrong read of the state (most dramatically: an
254	   entire verification round concluding "list items get zero decoration" when the real
255	   bug was simply that the test was running old code).
256	
257	5. **The sandboxed e2e vault and hand-built synthetic fixtures were not the real
258	   vault.** Real bugs (styles.css not loading at all; the fold-indicator collision,
259	   which only appears on list items with children; the marker-size bug, which only
260	   shows up when a heading and a paragraph are visible together) either didn't
261	   reproduce in the synthetic fixtures at all, or reproduced but weren't looked for.
262	   Testing exclusively against hand-picked, already-passing-shaped fixtures — rather
263	   than the user's actual notes, or a fixture corpus deliberately designed to be
264	   adversarial — kept validating the wrong thing.
265	
266	6. **A fully green automated test suite (198 unit + 33 e2e) was maintained by updating
267	   the tests to match whatever the current implementation did**, rather than encoding
268	   independent, fixed visual-correctness invariants that would fail if the
269	   implementation regressed. This means "all tests pass" never actually functioned as
270	   the safety net it appeared to be — it confirmed the code was internally consistent
271	   with itself, not that it was correct.
272	
273	## Carried-forward technical findings (implementation-agnostic)
274	
275	These hold regardless of what mechanism the next attempt uses:
276	
277	- `padding-left` moves content, never an element's own border/background box; anything
278	  with visible chrome (code fences, tables, callouts, quotes) needs `margin-left` or an
279	  equivalent box-moving property instead.
280	- Obsidian's native list hanging-indent is a `text-indent`(negative)+`padding-left`
281	  *pair*; if either half is overridden, both must be, together, deliberately, or the
282	  hang math breaks in ways that look like arbitrary misalignment.
283	- CSS custom properties resolve `em`/relative units at the point of *use*. Any rule
284	  meant to produce a *uniform* value across elements with different font-sizes (headings
285	  vs. body text) needs a `rem` (root-relative) or otherwise absolute unit — for every
286	  property that needs to be uniform, not just the one that was noticed first (this
287	  session fixed it for indentation and missed it for marker size in the same
288	  stylesheet).
289	- Obsidian's own `.cm-line` styles need higher specificity and often `!important` to
290	  override at all — confirmed repeatedly, not a one-off.
291	- The fold-indicator (collapse chevron) is present on any list item with children, is
292	  positioned at the same anchor as adjacent inline content, and extends a fixed pixel
293	  amount past that anchor independent of any custom indent scheme — a real collision
294	  hazard for any custom marker/overlay positioned near a list item's start.
295	- `obsidian-outliner`'s proven approach for guide lines is DOM measurement
296	  (`coordsAtPos`/`lineBlockAt`) plus absolutely-positioned overlay elements, not CSS
297	  padding/pseudo-element tricks — this session never actually implemented that approach
298	  despite researching it, and went with a CSS-only strategy throughout attempt 3, which
299	  is arguably the root cause of most of the fragility described above.
300	- This project's own prior research (`02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md`) already flagged
301	  the risk of a CSS-override-heavy strategy (the workflowy-style-outline case study,
302	  ~890 `!important` uses) before this change was ever proposed. That warning was
303	  available and was not weighed heavily enough before committing to the CSS-override
304	  strategy this session used throughout.
305	
306	## Open questions for the next iteration
307	
308	- Should guide lines and markers be implemented via DOM measurement + overlay elements
309	  (obsidian-outliner's proven pattern) instead of CSS padding/margin/text-indent
310	  overrides? This session never actually tried that path for anything beyond bullet
311	  glyph restyling.
312	- Is visual unification across encodings (headings/lists/paragraphs all reading as one
313	  indentation language) achievable at all without either (a) fully replacing native
314	  rendering for every kind — the path this project's own research already flagged as
315	  scorecard-risky — or (b) a measurement-based approach robust enough to survive
316	  arbitrary themes and fold-indicator-style native chrome this session didn't fully
317	  catalog?
318	- Is there a fundamentally different way to signal "this is one tree" that doesn't
319	  require in-place chrome matching at all — e.g., a structural overview pane, fold/zoom
320	  affordances, or something else entirely?
321	- What would a *real* verification protocol look like for this class of change — one
322	  that doesn't repeat the false-confidence patterns above? At minimum: screenshot a
323	  fixed, growing regression corpus (not just the fixture for the current fix) on every
324	  change; explicitly check *size* and *color* consistency across kinds, not just
325	  presence/position; test against the actual dev vault, not only synthetic fixtures;
326	  and treat a fully green test suite as necessary but not sufficient.
327	
328	## Current repository state
329	
330	The working tree has the full (broken) implementation uncommitted: `src/plugin/
331	decorate.ts`, `src/plugin/decorations.ts`, `styles.css`, the `main.ts` wiring, the e2e
332	spec, and updated OpenSpec artifacts for the `outline-decorations` change. Nothing has
333	been reverted — that decision (keep as a reference/starting point vs. discard) is left
334	for the next-iteration planning this document is meant to feed.
335

1	# Decoration experiments: plan (2026-07-13)
2	
3	Answers the postmortem's open question — is cross-kind visual unification achievable, and
4	how — by running small, isolated, falsifiable prototypes instead of another multi-attempt
5	scramble. See [06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md](06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md)
6	for what failed and why, and the git history of this doc's own introduction for the
7	external research that shaped this plan (obsidian-outliner's measure-don't-fight technique,
8	Logseq's per-block-component architecture, Silverbullet's declined-to-unify precedent).
9	
10	## Ground rules for every experiment below
11	
12	1. **One technique per experiment, isolated.** Each gets its own branch off a clean
13	   baseline (outline mode + grammar + structural ops, zero decoration code) so results are
14	   attributable and comparable, not entangled the way attempt 1→2→3 was last time.
15	2. **A fixed, shared fixture corpus, screenshotted every time — not just the fixture for
16	   the thing being fixed.** Every experiment runs against the *same* corpus (below), in
17	   both bundled light and dark themes. Growing the corpus is fine; shrinking scope of what
18	   gets screenshotted is not.
19	3. **A real-vault pass is mandatory before an experiment is called done**, not just the
20	   synthetic corpus. This was the single biggest false-confidence source last time.
21	4. **Record results in the comparison table at the bottom before moving to the next
22	   experiment.** Verdict is one of: keep, reject, or needs-follow-up — with the concrete
23	   reason.
24	5. **No experiment is "done" on green tests alone.** Unit tests here can only prove the
25	   pure computation is internally consistent; they cannot prove anything about rendering.
26	   Screenshot + real-vault verification is the actual gate.
27	
28	## The fixture corpus (shared across all experiments)
29	
30	- **Flat**: 3 top-level paragraphs, no lists or headings at all — the original motivating
31	  bug (`04-open-questions.md`: "with no bullets/indent chrome, outline mode is hard to
32	  verify by eye in flat documents").
33	- **Mixed**: heading → heading → nested bullet list → paragraph-as-sibling-of-list-item →
34	  code fence, matching `MIXED_MD` in the existing e2e spec.
35	- **Checkbox/task**: a top-level paragraph followed by a checkbox list, mixed
36	  checked/unchecked — the exact shape that broke in the field last time.
37	- **Heading-then-list, no paragraph**: a list directly under a heading with *no*
38	  intervening paragraph — isolates the "list item's native indentation doesn't know about
39	  heading ancestors" case, the crux of the additive-margin hypothesis.
40	- **Multi-line continuation**: a paragraph and a list item each spanning 2+ lines via
41	  `Shift+Enter` — never actually screenshotted last time; explicitly in scope now.
42	- **Wide numbering**: an ordered list crossing a digit-width boundary (items 9 and 10) —
43	  checks whether any supplemental indentation clashes with native marker-width variance.
44	- **Deep nesting**: 4+ levels mixing bullet and ordered markers.
45	
46	Committed once as [e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts](../../e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts) —
47	exported markdown-string constants (matching the project's existing `createNote(path,
48	content)` convention, not on-disk `.md` files) plus an `ALL_DECORATION_FIXTURES` array for
49	"screenshot everything" loops. Every experiment branch imports from here; don't fork or
50	re-type fixtures per experiment, or results stop being comparable.
51	
52	---
53	
54	## Experiment 1 (primary): additive-only indentation, no synthetic marker
55	
56	**Hypothesis**: your proposed compromise — drop the marker for non-list kinds entirely,
57	keep list items' native marker completely untouched, and get indentation right by *adding*
58	to what's there rather than *replacing* it.
59	
60	**Mechanism**:
61	- Paragraphs/headings/atoms: `padding-left` (atoms: `margin-left`) = `depth × unit`. Purely
62	  additive — nothing native to fight here, this part already worked in the D9 walk-back.
63	- List items: **never touch `text-indent`/`padding-left`.** Compute a new quantity —
64	  *supplemental depth* = total tree depth − depth-within-nearest-list-root (i.e. how many
65	  non-list-item ancestors sit above the nearest list root) — and apply `margin-left` =
66	  `supplemental-depth × unit` on top of native rendering. A list with no heading/paragraph
67	  ancestors above its root gets supplemental depth 0 → byte-identical to
68	  outline-mode-off, which becomes a permanent regression invariant, not a one-time check.
69	
70	**New code needed**: `decorate.ts`'s walk already computes total depth; add a second
71	counter that resets to 0 whenever a list-item ancestor chain starts, to get

185	### Experiment 1 — additive indentation, no marker
186	
187	**Verdict: keep.** All 8 corpus fixtures pass, both themes, screenshotted and visually
188	reviewed (not just DOM-asserted) — see
189	[e2e/specs/50-decorations.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/50-decorations.e2e.ts). The user toured
190	every note in the real `test-vault` by hand — the actual dev-vault pass the postmortem
191	insisted on, not a formality — and found three real bugs the synthetic corpus missed
192	entirely (below). All three are fixed, with permanent fixtures/assertions added so they
193	can't silently regress. User's assessment after the fixes: "much more promising" than the
194	prior attempt — indentation alone gives real hierarchy legibility switching modes on and
195	off, more than initially expected going in.
196	
197	**Code cost**: ~15 lines added to `decorate.ts` (2 new fields + 1 extra walk parameter),
198	~150-line `decorations.ts` adapter (a `StateField` for plain lines plus a `ViewPlugin` for
199	widget-replaced atoms), ~25-line `styles.css`.
200	
201	**Bugs found and fixed** (chronological — each was caught only by a human looking at real,
202	organic content, never by the synthetic corpus or DOM assertions):
203	
204	1. **Cascade fight, caught in the synthetic corpus.** Obsidian's own `app.css` has
205	   `.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > * { margin: 0px !important; }` — three
206	   classes of specificity, which beat our original two-class `.cm-line.to-decor-*` selector
207	   outright even with `!important` on both sides. Fixed by matching Obsidian's own
208	   ancestor-chain specificity, not by escalating `!important` further (there's nowhere
209	   further to escalate — the tie-break past equal `!important` is specificity, then source
210	   order).
211	2. **Tables and callouts not indenting at all, caught by the user in the real vault.**
212	   Obsidian renders tables, callouts, raw HTML blocks, and (surprisingly) horizontal rules
213	   as opaque replacement widgets in Live Preview (`.cm-embed-block`, or `.hr` for the rule,
214	   which oddly still carries a `cm-line` class despite being widget-rendered). A
215	   `Decoration.line` targeting that source line has **no effect at all** — not even a
216	   partial class-merge win, confirmed live by dumping the DOM: both class and inline style
217	   come back completely empty. Code fences and plain blockquotes are genuinely plain
218	   `.cm-line`s and were unaffected, which is exactly why the synthetic corpus (which only
219	   exercised those two atom kinds) never caught this. Fixed with a companion `ViewPlugin`
220	   that finds these widgets directly after each render (`docViewUpdate`, not the pre-render
221	   `update()` hook — DOM reads/writes need the widget to already exist) and sets
222	   `margin-left` inline with `!important`, which always wins over *any* stylesheet rule
223	   regardless of specificity — a clean escape hatch for exactly this class of native-widget
224	   cascade fight. It reads the same `--to-decor-unit` CSS variable the class-based rules
225	   use, so the unit constant stays single-sourced.
226	3. **Table still visibly offset from code/callout after fix #2, caught by the user.** Once
227	   tables got a margin at all, they still sat slightly further right than a same-depth code
228	   block or callout. Root cause: `.cm-embed-block.cm-table-widget` carries its own native
229	   left padding (for the row/column drag-handle icons) that callout's wrapper doesn't have.
230	   Padding never moves an element's own box — invisible for code/callout, whose *background*
231	   fills their whole padded box — but the table's actual visible content (a `<table>` nested
232	   two levels inside the padded wrapper) sits in normal flow within that padding, so it
233	   visually renders offset by exactly that amount. Fixed by reading the widget's own
234	   `getComputedStyle(...).paddingLeft` live (not a hardcoded pixel constant, which would be
235	   theme/version-fragile) and subtracting it from the applied margin, clamped at zero so a
236	   depth-0 atom never goes negative.
237	
238	**Deferred, not fixed on this branch**: list items sit visually further right than a
239	sibling paragraph/blockquote at the same tree depth. Traced to a real file
240	(`Kitchen Renovation.md`) and confirmed via measurement that our own added margin is
241	*identical* across all three at that depth — the extra offset is Obsidian's own native
242	list-item hang (`text-indent: -Npx` / `padding-left: Npx`), which already pushes list text
243	right of a sibling paragraph's text in **vanilla Obsidian, mode on or off**. It's not
244	something this experiment introduced; it's just more noticeable now that everything else
245	lines up precisely. Fixing it the way `obsidian-outliner`'s `BetterListsStyles` does means
246	touching that native `text-indent`/`padding-left` pair directly — the single riskiest
247	surface the postmortem blamed for most of the prior failed attempt's bugs. Left alone
248	pending a deliberate, isolated follow-up; see
249	[open question](#open-question-shrinking-only-our-own-added-list-margin) below for a
250	narrower variant that stays additive-only.
251	
252	Also confirmed **not** a bug: the flat fixture (3 top-level paragraphs, all depth 0) shows
253	no visual change from outline-mode-off. This was flagged going in as Experiment 3's
254	trigger condition, but the user confirmed on real content that this reads correctly the
255	moment a heading sits above the paragraphs — depth-0-with-no-ancestor genuinely has nothing
256	to convey, which is correct, not a legibility gap.

278	### Open question: shrinking only our own added list margin
279	
280	Raised by the user, not yet decided. The deferred list-hang issue (above) is native
281	Obsidian chrome and explicitly out of scope for direct edits in this experiment. But a
282	narrower variant stays inside the additive-only discipline: **reduce only the margin *we*

284	hang width, read live via `getComputedStyle` the same way the table fix (bug #3 above)
285	reads and compensates for native padding. Worth exploring as a follow-up, with two open
286	risks to resolve before trying it: (a) clamping so a shallow `supplementalDepth` never goes
287	negative once the hang is subtracted (the table fix's `max(0px, ...)` pattern applies
288	directly), and (b) the compensation must be based on the list **root**'s own hang, not each
289	item's — nested items can have wider markers (e.g. `10.` vs `-`) with different native hang
290	widths, and compensating per-item instead of per-root-chain would reintroduce exactly the
291	kind of within-list misalignment the wide-numbering fixture exists to catch.

293	## Non-obvious findings
294	
295	Cross-experiment learnings, carried forward the same way the postmortem's own "carried-forward
296	technical findings" section was meant to be used — read this before starting 2a/2b/3/4:
297	
298	- **CM6 `Decoration.line` has zero effect on Obsidian's "embed-block" replacement widgets**
299	  (tables, callouts, raw HTML, and horizontal rules) — not a partial win, not a class-merge;
300	  confirmed live that both class and inline style come back completely empty. Any atom kind
301	  Obsidian renders this way needs direct DOM patching via a `ViewPlugin`'s `docViewUpdate`
302	  hook, not a CM6 decoration. Code fences and plain blockquotes are *not* in this category —
303	  they render as genuinely plain `.cm-line`s and decorate normally.
304	- **A single broad selector plus a fact lookup by document line number is enough** to handle
305	  all four widget-replaced kinds uniformly (`.cm-embed-block, .cm-line.hr` → `posAtDOM` →
306	  `decorate()` facts by line) — no need for kind-specific branches.
307	- **Matching beats escalating.** When Obsidian's own CSS wins a specificity fight even
308	  against `!important`, the fix is matching its ancestor-chain specificity, not adding more
309	  `!important` (there's nowhere further to escalate past equal importance — the tie-break is
310	  specificity, then source order).
311	- **An inline style with `!important`, set via JS, always wins over any stylesheet rule**
312	  regardless of that rule's own specificity — a reliable escape hatch specifically for
313	  native-widget cascade fights that a CSS-only rule can't win.
314	- **Padding's visibility depends on where the background lives.** Padding never moves an
315	  element's own box (carried forward from the original postmortem), but whether that's
316	  visually invisible or visually offsetting depends on whether the element's own background
317	  fills the padded box (invisible, as for callout) or the visible content is nested one level
318	  further in without its own covering background (offsetting, as for a `<table>` inside a
319	  padded wrapper).
320	- **Read native values live instead of hardcoding compensations.** Subtracting a
321	  `getComputedStyle(...)`-read native padding from an own-added margin (clamped at zero)
322	  stays correct across themes/Obsidian versions; a hardcoded pixel constant would silently
323	  drift the moment a theme changes that padding.
324	- **List items already sit right of sibling text in vanilla Obsidian** — the native
325	  `text-indent`/`padding-left` hang pair reserves room for the bullet regardless of outline
326	  mode. This experiment's additive design correctly preserved that native behavior
327	  (verified: our own added margin is identical across list/paragraph/blockquote at the same
328	  depth); the extra offset users notice is pre-existing Obsidian behavior becoming more
329	  visible now that everything else aligns precisely, not a regression this experiment
330	  introduced.
331	- **The synthetic corpus, even a deliberately adversarial one, missed all three real bugs
332	  above.** Every one was caught only by a human visually reviewing real, organic vault
333	  content (not synthetic fixtures, not DOM-attribute assertions). This reaffirms the
334	  postmortem's central lesson directly, in a new implementation: treat the real-vault pass
335	  as load-bearing verification for every future experiment, not a final formality after the
336	  fixtures pass.

Now let me query DeepWiki for the exact reference implementation of obsidian-outliner's VerticalLines.ts, which the plan explicitly says to consult.

input.question: Show me the full implementation of VerticalLines.ts — how does it use view.coordsAtPos() and view.lineBlockAt() to compute pixel positions for guide lines? How does it create/manage the overlay div elements, how does it handle the layer/container outside .cm-content, what event triggers recompute (docChanged, viewportChanged, or something else), and how does it position each line (top/left/height) including for multi-line/wrapped content?

result.output:
{"result":"The `VerticalLines` feature in the `vslinko/obsidian-outliner` repository is responsible for drawing vertical indentation guide lines in the editor. It uses CodeMirror's `EditorView` methods `coordsAtPos()` and `lineBlockAt()` to calculate the pixel positions of these lines. The feature manages overlay `div` elements outside the `.cm-content` area and recalculates line positions on various editor updates.     

## Implementation Details of `VerticalLines.ts`

The core logic for rendering and managing vertical lines resides within the `VerticalLinesPluginValue` class, which is registered as a CodeMirror `ViewPlugin`.  

### Computing Pixel Positions with `view.coordsAtPos()` and `view.lineBlockAt()`

The `recursive` method within `VerticalLinesPluginValue` is responsible for calculating the `top`, `left`, and `height` of each vertical line. 

1.  **`left` position**: The `left` position of a line is determined by calling `this.view.coordsAtPos(fromOffset, 1)`.  The `fromOffset` represents the starting character offset of the list item's content. The `coords.right` property of the returned `DOMRect` is then used, adjusted by a `rootLeft` context value to ensure correct horizontal alignment. 
2.  **`top` position**: The `top` position is obtained using `this.view.lineBlockAt(fromOffset).top`.  This gives the pixel coordinate of the top of the line block containing the `fromOffset`. An adjustment is made if the line is outside the visible range. 
3.  **`height`**: The `height` of a vertical line is calculated by subtracting the `top` of the starting line block from the `bottom` of the ending line block.  The `tillOffset` is used to determine the end of the list or the next sibling.  For multi-line or wrapped content, `lineBlockAt()` correctly accounts for the full height of the visual line block, ensuring the vertical line spans the entire content.  The height is further adjusted by subtracting `1.5em` or `2em` depending on whether there's a next sibling. 

### Overlay `div` Elements and Container Management

The `VerticalLinesPluginValue` creates and manages two main `div` elements for the overlay:

1.  **`contentContainer`**: This `div` has the class `outliner-plugin-list-lines-content-container` and holds the individual vertical line elements. 
2.  **`scroller`**: This `div` has the class `outliner-plugin-list-lines-scroller` and acts as a scrollable container for the `contentContainer`.  It is appended directly to the `view.dom` (the editor's main DOM element), placing it outside the `.cm-content` area. 

The `updateDom` method dynamically creates or reuses `div` elements for each vertical line, assigning them the class `outliner-plugin-list-line` and setting their `top`, `left`, and `height` styles.  It also calculates the `height` and `marginLeft` for the `contentContainer` based on the editor's internal structure to ensure proper positioning relative to the editor's content. 

### Event Triggers for Recomputation

The recomputation of vertical line positions is triggered by several events:

*   **`update.docChanged`**: When the document content changes. 
*   **`update.viewportChanged`**: When the visible viewport of the editor changes (e.g., due to scrolling). 
*   **`update.geometryChanged`**: When the layout geometry of the editor changes. 
*   **`update.transactions.some((tr) => tr.reconfigured)`**: When the editor's configuration is reconfigured. 
*   **Settings Change**: The `scheduleRecalculate` method is also called when the plugin's settings change. 
*   **Scrolling**: An event listener is attached to `this.view.scrollDOM` to synchronize the `scroller`'s scroll position with the editor's scroll position.  

All these triggers lead to a call to `scheduleRecalculate`, which debounces the actual `calculate` operation using `setTimeout` to avoid excessive re-renders. 

## Notes

The `VerticalLines` feature is enabled only if the `verticalLines` setting is true and the default Obsidian theme is active.  The `VerticalLines` class itself is a `Feature` that registers the `ViewPlugin` and manages a body class `outliner-plugin-vertical-lines` to apply global styles.    The `styles.css` file defines the visual appearance of these lines, including `background-image` for the vertical line itself and `background-position-x` for its placement.

content.text: Let me verify CodeMirror 6's exact coordinate semantics for `lineBlockAt` vs `coordsAtPos` before implementing, to avoid guessing wrong on the trickiest part of this experiment.

input.question: What coordinate system do EditorView.lineBlockAt(pos).top/.bottom use — are they relative to the document top (scroll-position-independent) or relative to the viewport? Same question for EditorView.coordsAtPos(pos) — is that always viewport-relative (like getBoundingClientRect)? If I want to position an absolutely-positioned overlay div that lives outside .cm-content (e.g. appended as a sibling inside the scroller) so it scrolls naturally with the content, which coordinate system should I use for top/left, and do I need any offset/calibration against contentDOM's or scrollDOM's own bounding rect?

result.output:
{"result":"The user is asking about the coordinate systems used by `EditorView.lineBlockAt(pos).top/.bottom` and `EditorView.coordsAtPos(pos)`, and how to position an absolutely-positioned overlay div that scrolls with the content.

`EditorView.lineBlockAt(pos).top` and `.bottom` use coordinates relative to the top of the document, which is scroll-position-independent. `EditorView.coordsAtPos(pos)` returns viewport-relative coordinates, similar to `getBoundingClientRect()`. To position an absolutely-positioned overlay div that scrolls naturally with the content, you should use document-relative coordinates for `top` and `left`, and account for the `EditorView.documentTop` offset.

### Coordinate Systems

*   **`EditorView.lineBlockAt(pos).top/.bottom`**: These properties provide vertical positions relative to the top of the document . This means they are scroll-position-independent. The `EditorView.documentTop` getter provides the screen coordinates of the top of the document . This was explicitly added in version 0.19.22 to clarify that all height information is relative to this document top .
    *   The `lineBlockAt` method itself retrieves a `BlockInfo` object , which contains `top` and `height` properties . The `bottom` property is derived from `top + height` .
    *   The `lineBlockAt` method internally calls `viewState.lineBlockAt(pos)` , which in turn uses `heightMap.lineAt` to get the `BlockInfo` . The `HeightMap` is a tree structure that tracks the heights of lines and blocks in the document .

*   **`EditorView.coordsAtPos(pos)`**: This method returns coordinates that are viewport-relative . This is similar to `getBoundingClientRect()`. The method internally calls `docView.coordsAt(pos, side)` , which then retrieves coordinates from the underlying `Tile` structure .

### Positioning an Absolutely-Positioned Overlay

To position an absolutely-positioned overlay div that lives outside `.cm-content` (e.g., appended as a sibling inside the scroller) so it scrolls naturally with the content, you should use **document-relative coordinates** for `top` and `left`.

You will need to calibrate your overlay's position against the `EditorView.documentTop` .

Here's how you can achieve this:
1.  Get the document-relative vertical position of the content using `EditorView.lineBlockAt(pos).top` .
2.  Get the screen-relative top of the document using `EditorView.documentTop` .
3.  Calculate the `top` style for your overlay by adding the document-relative `top` from `lineBlockAt` to `EditorView.documentTop`.
4.  For the `left` position, you would typically use `EditorView.coordsAtPos(pos).left`  and then subtract the `scrollDOM`'s `left` offset to make it relative to the scroll container. The `getBase` function in `src/layer.ts` provides an example of calculating a base offset for positioning elements relative to the document within the scroll container .

Essentially, you want to convert the document-relative coordinates (from `lineBlockAt`) into viewport-relative coordinates, and then adjust them to be relative to your overlay's positioning context (e.g., the scroller).

## Notes

The `Glossary` wiki page provides a good overview of the core architectural entities and rendering concepts in `@codemirror/view` . Specifically, it defines `EditorView` as the central class for state-to-DOM transformation  and `ViewState` as tracking visual metrics like scroll position and line height . The `HeightMap` is crucial for efficiently calculating scroll positions and viewports .

Wiki pages you might want to explore:
- [Glossary (codemirror/view)](/wiki/codemirror/view#10)

View this search on DeepWiki: https://deepwiki.com/search/what-coordinate-system-do-edit_b85d0d5a-1b3b-4db9-ba5a-51ea60c82bf2
"}

content.text: CodeMirror 6 actually ships a first-party `layer()` extension built exactly for this (used internally for selection/cursor layers) — let me check its exact API since it may be a cleaner foundation than hand-rolling scroll calibration.

input.question: Show me the full API and an example usage of the `layer()` function/extension exported from @codemirror/view (src/layer.ts) — the LayerConfig type (above, class, update, markers, mount?), the RectangleMarker helper class if any, and how markers' draw()/update()/eq() work. Also show how an existing built-in feature (e.g. the selection match highlighter or the cursor layer) uses `layer()` in practice, since I want to draw absolutely-positioned rectangles (vertical guide lines) outside .cm-content that scroll naturally with the document, using view.coordsAtPos()/view.lineBlockAt() for positions.

result.output:
{"result":"You are asking about the `layer()` function/extension, the `LayerConfig` type, the `RectangleMarker` class, and how markers' `draw()`, `update()`, and `eq()` methods work, all exported from `@codemirror/view` . You also want to see an example of its usage, specifically how `drawSelection`'s `cursorLayer` or `selectionLayer` utilize `layer()` to draw absolutely-positioned rectangles that scroll with the document, using `view.coordsAtPos()` and `view.lineBlockAt()`  .

## `layer()` Function and `LayerConfig` Type

The `layer()` function is used to define extensions that draw DOM elements over or below the document text . It returns an `Extension` that creates a `ViewPlugin` to manage a DOM element for the layer and keep it synchronized with the editor's state .

The `LayerConfig` interface defines the configuration for a layer :
*   `above`: A boolean indicating whether the layer is shown above (`true`) or below (`false`) the text .
*   `class`: An optional CSS class to add to the DOM element that wraps the markers .
*   `update(update: ViewUpdate, layer: HTMLElement)`: A function called on every view update. Returning `true` triggers a marker update (a call to `markers` and redrawing of those markers) .
*   `updateOnDocViewUpdate`: An optional boolean that defaults to `true`. If `false`, the layer will not update every time the document view changes .
*   `markers(view: EditorView)`: A function that builds and returns an array of `LayerMarker` objects for the layer .
*   `mount?(layer: HTMLElement, view: EditorView)`: An optional function called when the layer is created .
*   `destroy?(layer: HTMLElement, view: EditorView)`: An optional function called when the layer is removed from the editor or the editor is destroyed .

## `LayerMarker` Interface and `RectangleMarker` Class

Markers shown in a layer must conform to the `LayerMarker` interface . These markers are created in a measuring phase and contain all their positioning information for drawing without further DOM layout reading . They are absolutely positioned relative to the document .

The `LayerMarker` interface requires the following methods :
*   `eq(other: LayerMarker): boolean`: Compares this marker to another marker of the same type to avoid unnecessary redraws .
*   `draw(): HTMLElement`: Returns an `HTMLElement` representing the marker .
*   `update?(dom: HTMLElement, oldMarker: LayerMarker): boolean`: An optional method to update an existing DOM element for this marker. It should return `true` if the update was successful and `false` if the element needs to be redrawn .

The `RectangleMarker` class is a standard implementation of `LayerMarker` that creates a `div` element at specified document-relative coordinates . Its dimension properties (`left`, `top`, `width`, `height`) are public .

*   **`constructor(className: string, left: number, top: number, width: number | null, height: number)`**: Creates a marker with a given class and dimensions .
*   **`draw()`**: Creates a `div` element, sets its class to `this.className`, and calls `this.adjust()` to set its style properties .
*   **`update(elt: HTMLElement, prev: RectangleMarker)`**: Updates the `HTMLElement` if the `className` is the same, otherwise returns `false` to indicate a redraw is needed . The `adjust` method sets the `left`, `top`, `width`, and `height` CSS properties .
*   **`eq(p: RectangleMarker)`**: Compares the `left`, `top`, `width`, `height`, and `className` properties to determine if two `RectangleMarker` instances are equal .
*   **`static forRange(view: EditorView, className: string, range: SelectionRange)`**: A static method that creates a set of `RectangleMarker` instances for a given selection range. For empty ranges, it creates a single rectangle at the cursor position. For non-empty ranges, it generates selection-style rectangles covering the content in a bidi-aware way . This method uses `view.coordsAtPos()` to get the coordinates .

## Example Usage: `drawSelection`'s `cursorLayer`

The `drawSelection` extension replaces the browser's native selection and cursor rendering with custom DOM elements . It uses `layer()` to create both a `cursorLayer` and a `selectionLayer` .

The `cursorLayer` is defined using `layer()` :
*   `above: true`: The cursor layer is drawn above the text .
*   `markers(view)`: This function iterates through all selection ranges in `view.state.selection.ranges` . For each empty range or if `drawRangeCursor` is enabled, it creates a `RectangleMarker` using `RectangleMarker.forRange(view, className, cursor)` . `view.coordsAtPos()` is implicitly used within `RectangleMarker.forRange` to determine the position of the cursor .
*   `update(update, dom)`: This function handles cursor blinking by toggling the `animationName` style property of the layer's DOM element when the selection changes . It also updates the blink rate if the configuration changes . It returns `true` if the document changed, selection changed, or configuration changed, triggering a marker update .
*   `mount(dom, view)`: Sets the initial blink rate when the layer is mounted .
*   `class: "cm-cursorLayer"`: Assigns a CSS class to the layer's DOM element .

The `selectionLayer` is similarly defined using `layer()` :
*   `above: false`: The selection layer is drawn below the text .
*   `markers(view)`: This function creates `RectangleMarker` instances for non-empty selection ranges using `RectangleMarker.forRange(view, "cm-selectionBackground", r)` . It also adds selection handles for iOS if configured .
*   `update(update, dom)`: Triggers a marker update if the document, selection, viewport, or configuration changes .
*   `class: "cm-selectionLayer"`: Assigns a CSS class to the layer's DOM element .

To draw absolutely-positioned rectangles (vertical guide lines) outside `.cm-content` that scroll naturally with the document, you would use `layer()` and provide a `markers` function that generates `RectangleMarker` instances. You can use `view.coordsAtPos()` to get the screen coordinates for a given document position  and `view.lineBlockAt()` to get information about a line block at a specific document position .

For example, to create vertical guide lines, your `markers` function would:
1.  Determine the document positions where you want your guide lines.
2.  For each position, call `view.coordsAtPos(position)` to get its screen coordinates.
3.  Use these coordinates to create `RectangleMarker` instances, specifying the desired `className`, `left`, `top`, `width`, and `height`. The `left` and `top` values should be adjusted relative to the layer's base position, which `RectangleMarker.forRange` handles internally by calling `getBase(view)`  .

## Notes

The `layer` function creates a `ViewPlugin` internally . The `LayerView` class manages the DOM element for the layer and handles the measuring and drawing of markers . The `dom` property of `LayerView` is the `div` element that wraps the markers, and it is appended to `view.scrollDOM` . This ensures that the layer scrolls with the document content. The `cm-layer` CSS class is applied to this wrapper element, which sets its `position` to `absolute` and `left`/`top` to `0` . Individual markers within the layer also have `position: absolute` .

1	/*
2	 * Outline mode decorations — additive-only indentation (Experiment 1, see
3	 * docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md and the postmortem it
4	 * links). `rem`, not `em`: custom properties resolve at the point of use,
5	 * so an `em`-based unit would inflate under a heading's larger font-size —
6	 * exactly the bug that shipped last time.
7	 *
8	 * Selector specificity: `!important` alone is not enough. Obsidian's own
9	 * app.css has `.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > * { margin: 0px
10	 * !important; }` (three classes) resetting margin on every `.cm-line` —
11	 * our `.cm-line.to-decor-*` (two classes) lost to it outright even with
12	 * `!important` on both sides, confirmed live against a real build. Matching
13	 * the same ancestor structure they use is what wins the cascade, not the
14	 * `!important` keyword by itself.
15	 */
16	
17	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-block {
18	  padding-left: calc(var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
19	}
20	
21	/* Atoms (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr) render a visible background or
22	 * border box; padding-left only shifts an element's own content, never
23	 * that box, so atoms need margin-left instead. */
24	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-atom {
25	  margin-left: calc(var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
26	}
27	
28	/* List items: native text-indent/padding-left (the bullet hang) is never
29	 * touched. margin-left is a box-model property native list rendering
30	 * doesn't otherwise use, so adding supplemental depth here is genuinely
31	 * additive on top of untouched native positioning. */
32	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-list {
33	  margin-left: calc(var(--to-supp-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
34	}

### Experiment 2a — guide lines via pixel-measured overlay

**Verdict: keep.** Branched off Experiment 1 (`experiment/decorations-2a-guides-overlay`).
All 8 corpus fixtures plus 4 real vault notes pass, both themes, screenshotted and visually
reviewed at pixel level (not just DOM-asserted) — see
[e2e/specs/51-guides-overlay.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/51-guides-overlay.e2e.ts), 42/42 e2e
tests green across the full suite (not just this spec). One ancestor-with-children gets one
continuous overlay `<div>` spanning from its first child's own line to its deepest
descendant's last line, positioned by *measuring* (`view.coordsAtPos`/`view.lineBlockAt`)
the ancestor's own already-rendered position rather than recomputing depth × unit —
obsidian-outliner's technique, applied to this project's universal (not list-only) tree.

**Deliberate deviation from the plan's literal wording**: the plan called for "a dedicated
`StateEffect`" for the out-of-band mode-toggle refresh. Dispatching a raw CM6 effect from
`main.ts` (application code, not a registered extension) has no path through Obsidian's
public `Editor` API — `EditorTransaction` exposes no `effects` field, and reaching into
`(editor as any).cm.dispatch()` is exactly the private-API surface this project's own
research already flags to avoid outside registered extensions
([03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md](03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md)). Used per-instance state
comparison instead (`ViewPlugin`/`StateField` each remember the outline-mode flag they last
saw and compare on every transaction — cheap, since it's just a boolean read, not a
reparse): functionally identical gate (`docChanged || viewportChanged || mode-just-toggled`),
zero private API. `main.ts`'s existing cursor-nudge dispatch (already public-API, unchanged)
still supplies the transaction that gives the comparison a chance to run.

**Code cost**: ~35 lines added to `decorate.ts` (`computeGuides`, a second tree walk),
~140-line addition to `decorations.ts` (a hand-rolled, debounced `ViewPlugin` reusing
`@codemirror/view`'s own exported `RectangleMarker` as a positioned-div factory, not CM6's
higher-level `layer()` helper — see the in-code rationale for why), ~20-line `styles.css`.

**Bugs found and fixed** (all three caught only by real e2e rendering/measurement — the
*pure* `computeGuides` unit tests were correct throughout; every bug was in the CM6
coordinate-system plumbing, exactly the kind of thing the postmortem warned unit tests
can't see):

1. **Guides rendered ~76px too high, caught by an e2e rect assertion, root-caused via live
   DOM diagnostics.** `view.lineBlockAt(pos).top/.bottom` are relative to `view.documentTop`
   (itself viewport-relative, moves with scroll) — not to the guide layer's own container
   origin (`scrollDOM`'s top-left, where the container's `top: 0; left: 0` anchors it). The
   two differ by a real, non-zero, confirmed-live constant (`.cm-content`'s own offset within
   the scroller). Fixed by converting through `documentTop` the same way `coordsAtPos`'s
   result already gets converted through a `getBase()`-style subtraction for the x-axis —
   the asymmetry (only x was being converted, not y) is what let this slip through code
   review.
2. **A list item's guide anchored ~12px right of its own bullet, caught by an e2e rect
   assertion against `.cm-formatting-list`.** `coordsAtPos(pos, 1)` ("after"-biased) at a
   line's very first position returns the *far* edge of the first character — past the
   bullet glyph, not at its start. `coordsAtPos(pos, -1)` ("before"-biased) gives the
   character's own left edge, matching the bullet's true rendered start.
3. **A doubly-nested list item's guide anchored a full level too far left, caught by the
   same assertion at a deeper fixture level.** A nested list item's raw source line *starts
   with its own indentation whitespace* (`"  - nested item"`); Obsidian visually collapses
   that leading whitespace via its native indent mechanism rather than rendering it at
   normal character width, so `coordsAtPos` at the raw line's first position (a space)
   lands at the *parent* list level's column, not the marker's. Fixed by skipping past
   `text.length - text.trimStart().length` characters before measuring — general, not
   list-item-specific (a no-op for headings/paragraphs, which have no leading whitespace).

All three were "invisible" in a full-page screenshot glance (subtle 1px, low-opacity lines)
and only surfaced by comparing exact measured coordinates — directly reinforcing the
postmortem's central lesson (DOM assertions test that code ran; here, pixel-level rect
assertions were what actually caught bugs a screenshot glance and a passing unit-test suite
both missed). A closer crop of one screenshot region did independently confirm the fix
visually once the coordinates were right.

**Multi-line continuation (the case never even attempted last time)**: confirmed via e2e
that `lineBlockAt`'s block-level `.top`/`.bottom` correctly span a Shift+Enter-continued
node's full rendered height "for free," for both a paragraph and a list item — no special
handling needed once the coordinate-conversion bugs above were fixed.

### Experiment 2b — guides via CSS stacked-gradient

Not started. Per the plan, this is meant to run head-to-head against 2a on the same
corpus (correctness on multi-line continuation, robustness against themes, code
size/complexity) before either is picked — not treated as a fallback.

input.new_string:
- **The synthetic corpus, even a deliberately adversarial one, missed all three real bugs
  above.** Every one was caught only by a human visually reviewing real, organic vault
  content (not synthetic fixtures, not DOM-attribute assertions). This reaffirms the
  postmortem's central lesson directly, in a new implementation: treat the real-vault pass
  as load-bearing verification for every future experiment, not a final formality after the
  fixtures pass.
- **CM6's coordinate systems are not interchangeable, and mixing them silently compiles.**
  `coordsAtPos()` is viewport-relative (like `getBoundingClientRect`); `lineBlockAt()`'s
  `top`/`bottom` are relative to `view.documentTop` instead — a *different* origin, even
  though both "sound" document-relative. Converting only one axis (x, via a `getBase()`-
  style subtraction) and not the other (y, left as the raw `lineBlockAt` value) type-checks
  fine and looks plausible in code review; it produced a ~76px vertical offset only caught
  by an e2e rect assertion against independently-measured `.cm-line` rects, never by the
  pure-function unit tests (which never touch real coordinates at all). Any experiment doing
  its own pixel measurement (2b's gradient positioning included, if it ends up needing any)
  should audit *every* axis's reference frame explicitly, not assume symmetry.
- **`coordsAtPos(pos, side)`'s side parameter matters at line starts, and defaults are not
  obviously safe.** `side: 1` ("after"-biased) at a line's very first position returns the
  far edge of the first character — for a list item, that's past the bullet glyph, not at
  its start. `side: -1` ("before"-biased) gives the character's own left edge. Untested,
  `side: 1` compiles and "looks right" (it did return *a* plausible-looking coordinate) —
  only a rect comparison against the actual rendered marker (`.cm-formatting-list`) caught
  the ~12px discrepancy.
- **A nested list item's raw source line starts with its own indentation whitespace, and
  Obsidian doesn't render that whitespace at literal character width.** Measuring
  `coordsAtPos` at a line's raw `.from` (before skipping leading whitespace) lands at
  wherever Obsidian's own indent mechanism collapses that whitespace to — which turned out
  to be the *parent* list level's own column, not the current line's marker. Any code
  measuring "where does this line's real content start" needs to skip
  `text.length - text.trimStart().length` characters first; this is general (headings/
  paragraphs have no leading whitespace, so it's a no-op there), not a list-item special case.
- **CM6 ships a first-party `layer()`/`RectangleMarker` pair (`@codemirror/view`, used
  internally for the selection/cursor layers) purpose-built for "measured overlay outside
  `.cm-content`, scrolls naturally."** `RectangleMarker` alone (just the positioned-div
  factory, not the full `layer()` wrapper) was reused here since `layer()`'s own `update()`
  hook only supports a synchronous redraw with no way to thread in the plan's requested
  debounce — but the full `layer()` extension is worth trying as-is for 2b or a future
  revisit if debouncing turns out not to matter in practice; it would remove the need for
  this experiment's manual scroll-coordinate calibration entirely.

content.text:
Everything's clean and gitignored appropriately. Here's a summary of the work.

## Summary

Implemented Experiment 2a (guide-line overlay) on a new branch `experiment/decorations-2a-guides-overlay`, off Experiment 1:

- **[decorate.ts](src/plugin/decorate.ts)**: added a pure `computeGuides()` walk producing one `GuideFact` per ancestor node with children (anchor line + vertical span), independent of indentation math.
- **[decorations.ts](src/plugin/decorations.ts)**: a hand-rolled, debounced (50ms) `ViewPlugin` that measures rendered positions via `coordsAtPos`/`lineBlockAt` and draws one `RectangleMarker`-based div per ancestor into a layer outside `.cm-content`. Refactored the existing indentation `StateField` to gate on `docChanged || mode-toggle` instead of every transaction (the plan's explicit ask).
- **Deliberate deviation**: used per-instance state comparison instead of a `StateEffect` for the mode-toggle refresh, since dispatching a raw CM6 effect from `main.ts` has no path through Obsidian's public `Editor` API and would require the private `.cm` reach this project's own research explicitly avoids. Documented in code and in the plan doc.

**Three real bugs found and fixed**, all via e2e pixel-measurement (never by unit tests or a screenshot glance):
1. Guides rendered ~76px too high — `lineBlockAt` and `coordsAtPos` use different coordinate origins; only one axis was being converted.
2. List-item guides anchored ~12px past the bullet — wrong `coordsAtPos` side bias.
3. Deeply-nested list guides anchored a full level too far left — measuring at the raw line's leading whitespace instead of skipping past it.

**Verification**: 210 unit tests, 42/42 e2e tests (full suite, both themes), screenshots reviewed across all 8 corpus fixtures plus 4 real vault notes — including a close crop to confirm a subtle depth-0 guide was actually rendering (it was). Results recorded in [07-decoration-experiments-plan.md](docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md).

Nothing committed — let me know if you'd like me to commit this or move on to Experiment 2b for the head-to-head comparison.

great. this looks pretty good in all cases except one: nested lists (no surprise).

* first, it messes with the built-in indentation guides functionality. when it's enabled, lists sometimes get double guides.
   * the built-in ones are nice because they go from one bullet marker to the next, precisely.
   * our guides at slightly off, they are sort of applied to lists as blocks, not really doing their job of guiding the indentation within the lists.
* second, even when the built-in guides are disabled, the new guides are off and are spaced unevenly. it seems like the deeper levels of a list are indented more that the first level is relative to its parent paragraph, so the guides make that unevenness more obvious.

I know we are not messing with the whole internal list spacing rebalancing. but our current case just doesn't really work with the lists. as an option, I would consider not adding guides for the nested lists and keeping the built-in guides. unless you have a plan for fixing it.

That matches my read of it too — once you look at the fixture screenshots, both problems trace back to the same cause: we're drawing our own guide for *every* ancestor including list-item ancestors, but within a single list chain, Obsidian's native list-indent-guides already do that precisely (bullet-to-bullet), while ours measure at a coarser, block-level granularity that doesn't respect the same per-level spacing. I don't think this is fixable by tuning our positioning — the two mechanisms are answering different questions (native: "where's the next bullet," ours: "where does this whole subtree hang from"). The clean fix is exactly what you suggested: stop drawing our guide when the ancestor itself is a list item, and let native handle all within-list nesting. We'd still draw our own guide for a heading/paragraph ancestor bridging *into* a list (something native has zero representation for), which is the case native can't help with. Let me implement that.

**Real-vault finding, fixed on this branch (not a separate follow-up)**: a *list-item*
ancestor gets no guide of its own, deliberately. The user's own real-vault comparison
(built-in indent guides only / both / ours only, screenshotted side by side) showed two
problems, both traced to the same cause: (a) with Obsidian's native "Show indent guides"
setting on, list nesting got visibly doubled lines; (b) even alone, our guides within a
list read as unevenly spaced, because native list nesting has its own internal per-level
width that our fixed unit doesn't match (the same native-hang mismatch Experiment 1 already
deferred rather than fight, now visible one layer up). Native indent guides already connect
one bullet precisely to the next *within* a list — there is no gap for a second mechanism to
usefully fill there. Fixed by having `computeGuides` skip pushing a guide when the anchor
node's `kind === 'list-item'`, while still including list-item subtrees in a *non-list*
ancestor's own span (a heading or paragraph bridging into a list still gets its guide,
reaching all the way through — only the within-list levels get none of their own). This
turns, e.g., the `deep-nesting` fixture (four levels, all list items) into zero guides at
all — entirely deferred to native — while `heading-then-list` keeps exactly one guide
(the heading's, spanning the whole list) instead of three.

content.text:
Working tree is clean (symlinks aren't tracked, so they don't show in git status). Everything's verified: typecheck, lint, 211 unit tests, and 43/43 e2e tests all green.

## Summary of the fix

Your diagnosis was right, and it pointed to the actual design flaw rather than a tuning issue: within a single list chain, Obsidian's native indent guides already do bullet-to-bullet precisely, and a block-level overlay guide alongside them can only either double up or look uneven — it's answering a different question ("where does this subtree hang from") than native answers ("where's the next bullet").

Fix in [decorate.ts](src/plugin/decorate.ts): `computeGuides` now skips producing a guide when the anchor node is a list item, while still including list subtrees in a *non-list* ancestor's span. So:
- `deep-nesting` (all list levels) → **zero guides now**, fully deferred to native.
- `heading-then-list` → **one guide** (the heading's, bridging into the whole list), not three.
- Your real journal note → one clean bridging line from the top-level paragraph, instead of the picket-fence of near-parallel lines.

Updated the unit tests, e2e spec (43/43 passing), and the plan doc's Experiment 2a write-up with this as a fixed real-vault finding (not a deferred follow-up), plus a note that Experiment 2b will face the identical native-coexistence question if you run that comparison later.

Want me to commit this, or keep iterating (e.g. on the real-vault check, or starting 2b)?

feat: outline-decorations Experiment 2a — pixel-measured guide overlay

Adds vertical guide lines that hang from each non-list ancestor down
through its subtree, measured via coordsAtPos/lineBlockAt rather than

hang or per-kind indentation quirks.

native indent guides already connect one bullet precisely to the next
within a list, and running both at once either doubles lines or (ours
alone) reads as unevenly spaced against native list-internal spacing —
a real-vault finding, not a hypothetical. Bridging guides (heading or
paragraph down into a list) are unaffected, since native has no
representation for that case at all.
